bytes.topic: Database Selection — OLTP vs OLAP, relational vs document vs wide-column vs search
bytes.title: Elasticsearch — Search Engine First, Not a Database Replacement
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch is built on an inverted index, not a B-tree or LSM-tree. That architectural choice defines exactly where it fits in your stack — and where it doesn't.

It wins at full-text search with BM25 relevance scoring, near-real-time log analytics via `date_histogram` aggregations, faceted search combining `bool` filters with `terms` aggs, and vector/semantic search through `knn` queries on `dense_vector` fields. It loses hard at OLTP: there are no ACID transactions, writes aren't searchable until after a refresh cycle (default 1s), and reads are eventually consistent — a coordinating node may return a stale shard.

Relational joins are absent by design. `nested` objects and `parent-child` relationships exist but carry serious query-time costs at scale. For pure columnar analytics, ClickHouse will outperform Elasticsearch on throughput by orders of magnitude.

The right mental model: Elasticsearch is a **search layer**, not a source of truth. Pair it with PostgreSQL for authoritative state and authoritative writes. Sync to Elasticsearch via CDC or a queue for the search and aggregation surface. Pick it when your query pattern is search-first — relevance, ranking, or log grep-and-aggregate.

bytes.topic: PostgreSQL — ACID transactions, indexes, JSONB, partitioning, extensions (pgvector)
bytes.title: Elasticsearch vs PostgreSQL JSONB — When GIN Indexes Beat Your Inverted Index
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch's near-real-time search has a 1-second index refresh gap — writes aren't visible until the next refresh cycle. PostgreSQL with JSONB and GIN indexes eliminates that gap entirely with ACID-consistent full-text search.

A GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) on a JSONB column is Elasticsearch's inverted index equivalent, but inside a transaction. You get the same O(log n) term lookups via @@ full-text operators or @> containment queries, but reads always reflect the latest committed write — no refresh_interval tuning required.

Partition the table by created_at using range partitioning and PostgreSQL's partition pruning mirrors Elasticsearch's time-based index rollover strategy. Add pgvector's HNSW index on an embedding column alongside the GIN index, and a single SQL query handles both keyword and semantic search — no cross-cluster joins between Elasticsearch and a separate vector store.

The tradeoff is real: GIN indexes are slower to update than Elasticsearch's bulk segment merges. For write-heavy pipelines ingesting above 10k docs/sec, Elasticsearch's refresh model wins. Below that threshold, PostgreSQL's transactional consistency and zero operational overhead make it the sharper tool.

bytes.topic: Cassandra — partition key design, wide rows, eventual consistency, data modeling for queries
bytes.title: Cassandra Partition Key Design — Avoiding Hot Spots and Modeling for Your Queries
bytes.body:
Cassandra's data model starts with your queries, not your entities. Every table is a denormalized, query-optimized view — normalization is an anti-pattern.

Partition keys control data distribution across nodes. A bare `user_id` partition key for a high-traffic account routes all writes to one node, creating a hot spot. Composite keys like `(user_id, month)` cap partition size and spread load across the ring. Target partitions under 100MB — beyond that, compaction and repair performance degrade significantly.

Clustering columns define sort order within a partition, enabling efficient range scans: `WHERE user_id = ? AND month = ? AND activity_time > ?` is a single-partition read — fast and cheap. Cross-partition queries are scatter-gather operations that fan out to multiple nodes and should be avoided on hot paths.

Consistency is tunable per statement. `QUORUM` writes/reads achieve "strong" consistency at latency cost. `ONE` maximizes throughput with staleness risk. For linearizable reads — inventory, counters — use `SERIAL` (Paxos-backed lightweight transactions).

One query pattern → one table. Two access patterns on the same data? Maintain two tables. Disk is cheap; cross-partition coordination is not.
bytes.code_snippet:
CREATE TABLE user_activity (
  user_id   UUID,
  month     TEXT,
  ts        TIMESTAMP,
  action    TEXT,
  metadata  TEXT,
  PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, month), ts)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ts DESC)
  AND default_time_to_live = 2592000;

-- Single-partition range scan — O(log n) within partition
SELECT action, metadata
  FROM user_activity
 WHERE user_id = :uid
   AND month   = '2024-01'
   AND ts      > '2024-01-15 00:00:00'
LIMIT 50;

bytes.topic: Elasticsearch — inverted index, mapping, analyzers, query DSL, aggregations
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Analyzers — Why Your Queries Miss Exact Matches
bytes.body: When you index a document, Elasticsearch runs each text field through an analyzer pipeline: character filters → tokenizer → token filters. The default `standard` analyzer lowercases and tokenizes on whitespace/punctuation, storing tokens in the inverted index — not the raw string. This means a mapping defined as `"type": "text"` with no custom analyzer will tokenize `"Apache Kafka"` into `["apache", "kafka"]`. A `term` query against `"Apache Kafka"` returns nothing because it bypasses analysis and expects an exact token match. Use `match` queries for analyzed fields — they run the same analyzer at query time. For exact lookups (IDs, enums, status fields), map them as `"keyword"`. For fields needing both, use multi-fields: `text` for full-text search, `keyword` sub-field for exact match and aggregations. Aggregations like `terms` always operate on `keyword` — running them on a `text` field raises a `fielddata` error unless you explicitly enable it (avoid this in production). Audit your mappings with `GET /index/_mapping` before diagnosing query bugs.

bytes.topic: ClickHouse — columnar storage, materialized views, MergeTree engine, OLAP queries
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Engineers: Why ClickHouse's MergeTree Engine Wins for OLAP
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch handles aggregations by scanning documents across shards — workable for log analytics but expensive at scale. ClickHouse's MergeTree engine stores data column-by-column on disk: a GROUP BY log_level query reads only that column, skipping everything else. Compare that to ES's doc-values, which still load per-document field data for every matching hit.

Where ES uses inverted indices and BKD trees for search, ClickHouse uses sparse primary key indices with mark files for range scans. For OLAP workloads — summing metrics, counting distinct users, bucketing events by hour — ClickHouse's columnar layout means 10–100x less I/O than Elasticsearch.

Materialized views in ClickHouse behave like ES Transform jobs but execute synchronously on INSERT. Pair one with a SummingMergeTree target table to pre-aggregate at write time — reads become near-instant because the math already happened.

Mental model for ES engineers: ES index ≈ ClickHouse table, ES shard ≈ ClickHouse partition, ES segment merge ≈ ClickHouse part merge (background merges that deduplicate and collapse aggregated rows). ClickHouse won't replace Elasticsearch for full-text search, but for time-series OLAP at scale, it operates in a different efficiency class entirely.

bytes.topic: TimescaleDB — hypertables, continuous aggregates, compression, time-series queries
bytes.title: Elasticsearch TSDS — TimescaleDB Concepts Mapped to Native ES Time-Series
bytes.body:
TimescaleDB's hypertable auto-partitions by time under the hood. Elasticsearch's equivalent is a Time Series Data Stream (TSDS), GA since 8.7. Set `index.mode: time_series` on your index template and declare a `routing_path` — your dimension fields like `host.name` or `service.name`. ES hashes these into a `_tsid` and co-locates all points for a given series on the same shard, giving you the same read locality that TimescaleDB guarantees per chunk.

For continuous aggregates, reach for ES Transforms: schedule a `date_histogram` + metric pivot on your TSDS index and materialize results into a rollup index. Dashboard queries hit the rollup — not the raw stream.

TSDSs apply a synthetic `_source` that physically stores metrics in a columnar layout automatically — no `best_compression` codec wrangling. Pair with ILM cold/frozen tiers to replicate TimescaleDB's `compress_after` and `drop_after` retention policies.

`time_bucket('1 hour', time)` maps directly to a `date_histogram` with `calendar_interval: 1h`. The mental model transfers cleanly once you know which ES primitive owns each TimescaleDB concept.

bytes.topic: Indexing Strategy — B-tree vs hash vs GIN vs BRIN, covering indexes, partial indexes
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Indexing Strategy — Inverted Index, BKD Trees, doc_values, and When to Disable Them
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch uses different internal index structures depending on field type. Text fields build an inverted index — token → doc list — optimized for full-text search. Numeric, date, and geo fields use BKD trees (block k-d trees), which make range queries and geo-distance filters fast without scanning all docs.

For aggregations and sorting, Elasticsearch relies on doc_values — a columnar on-disk structure built at index time for keyword, numeric, and date fields. It's the equivalent of a covering index: data is pre-organized for retrieval without fetching _source. Disabling doc_values saves disk but kills sort/agg on that field.

Set index: false on fields you store but never query (e.g. raw payload blobs). Set doc_values: false on high-cardinality text fields used only for search. For text fields needing aggregations, avoid fielddata: true — it loads the entire uninverted index into heap. Instead, use a keyword sub-field.

Index sorting (index.sort.field) physically orders segments by a field, making early termination possible on top-N queries — analogous to a BRIN-style range optimization.

bytes.topic: Query Optimization — EXPLAIN ANALYZE, slow query log, N+1, query planning
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Query Optimization — Profile API, Slow Logs, and Killing N+1 Fetches
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch's Profile API is your EXPLAIN ANALYZE equivalent. Wrap any query in `"profile": true` and you get shard-level timing broken into `build_scorer`, `next_doc`, `match`, and `score` phases. A `build_scorer` time dominating the breakdown usually means a costly script or wildcard leading clause — rewrite with a `terms` filter or a `keyword` field instead.

For persistent visibility, set `index.search.slowlog.threshold.query.warn: 500ms` per index or cluster-wide via `_cluster/settings`. Slow logs surface real traffic patterns Profile can't capture.

The classic Elasticsearch N+1 pattern: fetching a list of IDs from one index, then issuing individual `GET /{index}/_doc/{id}` calls in a loop. Replace with a single `_mget` or a `terms` query with `_source` filtering. For deep pagination, swap `from/size` for `search_after` — `from: 10000` forces Elasticsearch to collect and discard 10k hits on every shard before returning results, destroying latency at scale.

bytes.topic: Data Modeling — normalization vs denormalization, schema design for access patterns
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Data Modeling — Denormalize First, Use Nested and Join Sparingly
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch is document-oriented — denormalization is the default and correct choice for most access patterns. Flattening related data into a single document avoids expensive cross-document lookups and keeps query paths simple.

The trap: plain `object` arrays. Elasticsearch flattens object arrays internally, destroying field correlation. A query for `{color: red, size: L}` will match a doc containing `[{color: red, size: S}, {color: blue, size: L}]` — a silent false positive.

Use `nested` type when you need to query correlated fields within array elements. Each nested object is stored as a hidden Lucene document and queried via `nested` query + `path`. It preserves correctness but increases index size and query overhead.

For true one-to-many where the child side updates frequently and independently, `join` field (parent-child) avoids reindexing the parent on every child update — but carries a ~5–10x query penalty and requires all related docs on the same shard.

Design rule: start fully denormalized, add `nested` when array correlation produces wrong results, reach for `join` only when child update frequency makes reindexing the parent prohibitive.

bytes.topic: Transactions & ACID — isolation levels, optimistic vs pessimistic locking
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Concurrency — Optimistic Locking with _seq_no and _primary_term
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch has no multi-document transactions or SQL isolation levels — writes are atomic only at the single-document level. For concurrent writes, it uses optimistic concurrency control via `_seq_no` and `_primary_term`. Every document carries these values; pass them as `if_seq_no` and `if_primary_term` on index, update, or delete requests. If another write modified the document since your read, Elasticsearch returns a `409 VersionConflictEngineException` — re-fetch and retry.

Pessimistic locking doesn't exist. There's no `SELECT FOR UPDATE` equivalent. The only pattern is: read → modify → write with version params → handle 409. For high-contention documents, implement retry logic with exponential backoff.

The old `_version` field (ES ≤ 6) is deprecated — always use `_seq_no` + `_primary_term`, which are shard-level monotonic counters reliable across distributed primaries.

One critical gotcha: `GET /<index>/_doc/<id>` is read-your-writes consistent (reads from the translog), but `POST /<index>/_search` only reflects the last segment refresh (1s default). A successful write is not immediately searchable.

bytes.topic: Replication — leader/follower, multi-leader, leaderless, consistency trade-offs
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Replication — Primary-Replica, CCR, and Consistency Trade-offs
bytes.body: Elasticsearch uses a primary-replica model within a cluster: every shard has exactly one primary (tracked by `_primary_term`) that processes all writes and fans them synchronously to replicas before acknowledging. This is strictly leader/follower — no leaderless quorum writes like Cassandra. For cross-cluster replication, CCR formalizes this pattern with explicit leader and follower indices: the follower cluster polls the leader's internal `shard_changes` API, replaying operations in sequence-number order. Follower lag is observable via `GET /_ccr/stats` under `leader_global_checkpoint` vs `follower_global_checkpoint`. The critical consistency knob is `index.write.wait_for_active_shards`: `all` gives strong durability (write fails if any replica is unavailable), `1` (default) favors availability at the cost of potential data loss on hard failure. There is no multi-leader mode — CCR is read-scale only. Writing to a follower index is blocked by design. For safe failover, always use `if_seq_no` + `if_primary_term` on updates to prevent lost-write anomalies when a primary is reassigned during a network partition.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a CCR follower index (run on the follower cluster)
PUT /logs-follower/_ccr/follow
{
  "remote_cluster": "us-east",
  "leader_index": "logs-leader",
  "max_read_request_operation_count": 5120,
  "max_outstanding_read_requests": 12,
  "read_poll_timeout": "5m"
}

# Harden write durability on the leader
PUT /logs-leader/_settings
{ "index.write.wait_for_active_shards": "all" }

# Monitor replication lag
GET /_ccr/stats

bytes.topic: Sharding — horizontal partitioning, shard key selection, hot spots
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Sharding — Routing Keys, Hot Spots, and Partition Count
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch routes every document to a shard using: `shard = hash(_routing) % number_of_primary_shards`. By default, `_routing` equals `_id`, giving even distribution but no data locality — queries fan out to all shards.

Custom routing groups related documents together. Index a log with `?routing=tenant-42` and all queries filtered by that tenant hit a single shard. Fast. But with only 3 high-traffic tenants across 10 shards, 7 shards sit idle while 3 get hammered — a classic hot spot.

`index.routing_partition_count` (must be < `number_of_shards`) spreads each routing key across a partition subset instead of one shard. With `routing_partition_count: 3`, `tenant-42` maps to 3 shards, not 1 — balancing locality with distribution. The mapping must mark `_routing` as required or the partition math breaks silently.

Diagnose hot spots with `GET /_cat/shards?v&h=shard,docs,store&s=store:desc`. Healthy clusters show roughly equal doc counts per shard; severe skew causes uneven query latency and OOM risk on the hot node.

Never change `number_of_shards` post-creation — it invalidates all routing calculations. Size correctly upfront and use the Rollover API with ILM for time-series indices.

bytes.topic: Backup & Recovery — point-in-time recovery, backup strategies, RTO vs RPO
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Snapshots — Nail Your RTO and RPO Without Guessing
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch's snapshot API is your primary backup mechanism. Snapshots are incremental by default — only changed segments since the last snapshot are written, making frequent snapshots cheap. Register a repository first (S3, Azure Blob, GCS, or shared filesystem), then snapshot on a schedule using SLM (Snapshot Lifecycle Management).

RPO (Recovery Point Objective) maps directly to your SLM schedule frequency. A 1-hour schedule means you can lose up to 1 hour of data. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) depends on shard count, index size, and network throughput to your snapshot repo — test restores regularly; a 100GB index on S3 with 10 shards restores in minutes, not hours.

For point-in-time recovery, combine snapshots with index-level `_pit` (Point In Time) searches for read consistency, but actual data recovery still goes through snapshot restore. Use `indices.recovery.max_bytes_per_sec` to throttle restore throughput so production traffic isn't starved. Always restore into an alias — swap the alias post-restore so the cutover is instant and rollback is trivial.

bytes.topic: Connection Pooling — PgBouncer, connection limits, pool sizing
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Connection Pooling — Sizing Your Transport Layer Right
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch clients manage HTTP connection pools per node via the transport layer — no external pooler like PgBouncer applies here. In elasticsearch-py, each node gets its own urllib3 `HTTPConnectionPool`. The `connections_per_node` parameter caps simultaneous HTTP connections per node; the default of 10 is often too low for bulk-indexing workloads.

Undersizing the pool causes connection queuing inside urllib3 — requests block waiting for a free socket rather than failing fast or load-balancing. Oversizing wastes file descriptors and can flood the coordinating node's HTTP thread pool, triggering `es_rejected_execution_exception`.

Sizing heuristic: `connections_per_node = ceil(worker_threads / node_count) + 2`. Enable `sniff_on_start=True` so the client discovers all data nodes rather than funneling load through a single seed. After resizing, validate against `GET /_cat/thread_pool/write?v&h=name,active,queue,rejected` — if `queue` is non-zero, your ES-side thread pool is the bottleneck, not the client pool. Tune `thread_pool.write.queue_size` in `elasticsearch.yml` alongside client `connections_per_node` for end-to-end headroom.
bytes.code_snippet:
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch

es = Elasticsearch(
    hosts=["http://es-node1:9200", "http://es-node2:9200"],
    connections_per_node=20,     # urllib3 pool size per node
    sniff_on_start=True,          # discover full cluster topology
    sniff_on_node_failure=True,   # re-sniff after a node drop
    retry_on_timeout=True,
    max_retries=3,
    request_timeout=30,
)

# Inspect active HTTP connections and thread-pool pressure
print(es.cat.nodes(v=True, h="ip,name,http.current_open"))
print(es.cat.thread_pool("write", v=True, h="name,active,queue,rejected"))

bytes.topic: Security — encryption at rest/transit, row-level security, least privilege
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Security — Encryption, DLS/FLS, and Least-Privilege Roles
bytes.body: Elasticsearch's X-Pack security stack covers three layers you must harden before production. Transport encryption requires enabling TLS on both the HTTP layer (`xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true`) and the inter-node transport layer (`xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true`) — skipping the transport layer leaves node gossip unencrypted even if your REST API is behind HTTPS. Encryption at rest isn't built into ES itself; you rely on OS-level encrypted volumes (dm-crypt/LUKS, Azure Disk Encryption) combined with the Elasticsearch keystore (`bin/elasticsearch-keystore`) to protect secrets like S3 repository credentials. For row-level access, ES uses Document-Level Security (DLS) and Field-Level Security (FLS) inside role definitions. DLS filters which documents a role can see via a query; FLS controls which fields are returned. Pair these with the principle of least privilege: create dedicated roles per service, never reuse the `elastic` superuser, and audit access with the `xpack.security.audit.enabled: true` flag. The `_security/role` API lets you define all of this programmatically.

bytes.topic: Migrations — zero-downtime migrations, backward compatibility, tooling
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Zero-Downtime Migrations with Aliases and the Reindex API
bytes.body:
Elasticsearch has no ALTER TABLE. Schema changes — adding analyzers, changing field mappings, switching from `text` to `keyword` — require a full index rebuild. The zero-downtime pattern hinges on aliases.

Create `products_v1` and point the `products` alias at it. When you need a mapping change, create `products_v2` with the updated mappings, then use the `_reindex` API to copy documents over. Set `slices: auto` to parallelize across shards. While reindex runs, your app reads and writes through the `products` alias — uninterrupted.

Once reindex completes, atomically swap the alias in a single `_aliases` call using `add` + `remove` in one request — Elasticsearch guarantees this is atomic. Mark `products_v2` as the `is_write_index` before the cutover to route new writes there immediately.

For backward compatibility during rollout, keep both indices behind the alias and use `_source` filtering or runtime fields to normalize shape differences. Delete `products_v1` only after confirming no rollback is needed.

Tooling: Elasticsearch's `_migration` helper API (7.x+) flags deprecated field usages before you upgrade clusters.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — slow queries, connection counts, replication lag, storage growth
bytes.title: Elasticsearch Monitoring — Slow Queries, Connection Counts, Replication Lag, and Storage Growth
bytes.body: Elasticsearch exposes cluster health through the `_cat` and `_nodes` APIs — use them to catch problems before they cascade. Slow queries are logged via the `index.search.slowlog.threshold.query.warn` setting (e.g., `5s`); ship these logs to a dedicated index for aggregation. For connection counts, `GET /_nodes/stats/http` returns `current_open` and `total_opened` per node — spikes in `current_open` signal connection leaks in your client pool. Replication lag isn't exposed directly, but `GET /_cat/shards?v` shows unassigned or initializing replicas; pair it with `_cluster/pending_tasks` to detect replication bottlenecks. Storage growth lives in `GET /_cat/allocation?v`, which breaks down `disk.used`, `disk.avail`, and `shards` per node — alert when any node crosses 75% (Elasticsearch's own watermark is 85%). For a unified view, poll `GET /_nodes/stats` and feed `indices.store.size_in_bytes`, `http.current_open`, and `jvm.mem.heap_used_percent` into Prometheus via `elasticsearch_exporter`.
bytes.code_snippet:
GET /_nodes/stats/http,indices,jvm

# Key fields to extract and alert on:
# nodes.<id>.http.current_open        → active connections
# nodes.<id>.indices.store.size_in_bytes → storage per node
# nodes.<id>.jvm.mem.heap_used_percent   → GC pressure

GET /_cat/shards?v&h=index,shard,prirep,state,unassigned.reason
# prirep=r + state=UNASSIGNED → replication lag indicator

GET /_cat/allocation?v&h=node,disk.used,disk.avail,disk.percent,shards
# Alert threshold: disk.percent > 75
